turns.content: let me tell you something friend... I walked through Kadıköy today, my neighbourhood, and I did not recognize half the street
That must be a strange feeling, especially in a place you've known for decades. What's changed that caught your eye?

the old ironmonger on Muvakkithane street... gone. finished. now it is a... what do they call it, a 'concept café.' they sell coffee with lavender in it. lavender! in coffee! the ironmonger was there since before I was born, 50 years maybe more. now... lavender coffee

Ha — lavender coffee replacing a 50-year-old ironmonger pretty much captures the whole gentrification trend in one image. It's happening in historic neighbourhoods everywhere, not just Istanbul. The businesses that served the community for generations get replaced by things that serve a completely different crowd.

Is it mostly the commercial streets changing, or is it deeper than that?

deeper... much deeper. the people are different. when I was young, Kadıköy was... how do I say this. it was real. fishermen at the harbour in the morning, the spice sellers shouting, the old Armenian bakery on Güneşlibahçe... you could smell the bread from three streets away. the women shopping with their baskets, not with delivery apps. it was alive, you know... it breathed

You're describing a neighbourhood that had a rhythm — a living, breathing ecosystem of tradespeople and residents who all knew each other. That kind of organic community is really hard to replicate once it's gone. The Armenian bakery, the spice sellers, the fishermen — those aren't just businesses, they're the texture of daily life.

When did you start noticing the shift?

maybe 2010... 2012. slowly at first. one bar here, one gallery there. then the rents started climbing and suddenly... pfff. the shoemaker closes, becomes a cocktail bar. the tailor closes, becomes a vintage shop. the cheese seller... becomes a place that sells only avocado toast. avocado! we do not even grow avocados in Turkey

The pattern is always the same — it starts with a few "interesting" newcomers, which raises the profile, which raises the rents, which pushes out the tradespeople who made the neighbourhood interesting in the first place. Then you end up with a street full of trendy places that could be anywhere in the world.

An avocado toast shop in a neighbourhood known for its cheese sellers is almost comically ironic. Are any of the old-school shops still hanging on?

some... the fish market is still there, alhamdulillah. Bülent the butcher is still there, his father was there before him. and of course me... I am still here. but every year there are fewer of us. we are like... the old trees in a forest that they are cutting down to build a parking lot. still standing but... for how long

That's a vivid image — old trees in a disappearing forest. The fact that you, Bülent, and the fish market are still there means the neighbourhood hasn't completely lost its soul yet. You're the continuity, the living memory of what Kadıköy was.

Do the new shops and the old ones coexist at all, or is it totally separate worlds?

separate worlds... mostly. the young people who come to the cafés, they do not come to me for a shave. they go to these new 'barber shops' with the neon signs and the hip hop music and the 300 lira haircuts. but you know what... I do not mind so much. my customers are loyal. they come because they know what a real shave is. 'eski dostlar yeni dostlardan iyidir'... old friends are better than new friends

That's a good proverb for the situation. And honestly, those neon-sign barber shops come and go — they're trend businesses. A 30-year shop built on craft and loyalty outlasts all of them. Your customers aren't coming for the aesthetic, they're coming for the experience and the trust.

Does it ever make you sad, though? Walking through and seeing how much has changed?

of course it does, friend. of course. I remember when Kadıköy was a place where everyone knew your name. now I walk and I am a stranger on my own street. but you know... in German they say 'die Zeit heilt alle Wunden'... time heals all wounds. maybe it also creates them, hah. anyway... the neighbourhood changes but the barber stays. that is enough for me. I should go, it is almost time to open the shop. the old trees do not take days off

Ha — the old trees don't take days off. I like that. You're right that time works both ways — it heals and it changes things beyond recognition. But as long as your shop is there, Kadıköy still has a piece of what it was. Go open up. The neighbourhood needs you.
